Closing due to inactivity. Still, I welcome contributions to this end.

In the [example bot provided in the source](/irc/bot.py), the bot keeps track of each user in each channel, and when a user quits, the internal copy of the users that channel was in are removed. The IRC client doesn't have a special connection to the server, so has no way to signal to get a channel list for the user prior to the quit being processed. Therefore, the technique in irc/bot.py is probably your best bet.

Original comment by: Jason R. Coombs

I just did a little experiment on this:

1. Go to generator, right click -> add empty message
2. set modulation to FSK
3. set pause to 0
4. Play that (__one sending repeat__) -> Can hear a sound. I also exported the signal using the  "Generate file" as a ``` .wav ``` and played it via VLC which produced the same sound

Now I changed the bit length of the modulation to __10__. I could __not__ hear a sound in the generator with one sending repeat. I also exported the file as  ``` .wav ``` again to play it with VLC which also remained silent.

So I think the problem here is the short bit length and the resulting short sample sequences. I suspect the sound card needs a minimum of data to turn on or the generated sound is too short for the human ear. I am not an audio expert though, but as VLC also does not play a sound I am quite confident that this is not a bug in URH.

Exons of tens of bp will be hard. One thing you could try is to reduce the intron-open cost with `-q`:
```sh
minimap2 -ax cdna -k14 -q 2,40 -G 50000
```
where the first number is the gap-open cost and the second number is the intron-open cost in effect. In the context of rna-seq mapping, `-G` is the max intron length. It is set to 100kb by default. I guess A. thaliana does not have many large introns, so suggested a smaller number. Documentations will be updated when this cdna branch gets more mature.

> I actually really like the idea of representing the splices as long deletions in the sequence by the way

The latest "cdna" branch will represent introns with the "N" CIGAR operator. This, I believe, is the convention among RNA-seq mappers. Nonetheless, it is also easy to add a command-line option to switch between "N" and "D" for introns.

This is python's build type feature, not bug.

In Python, `(x)` means `x`, but `(x,)` means a `tuple type contains value x`.

For checking one argument, you can write in this way.

```python
assert spyB.args == [(1,)]
```

[Here is some answer from StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12876177/how-to-create-a-tuple-with-only-one-element)

> The existing plot_decision_regions function has built in validation and tests to ensure that there aren't more than 2 training features (which is really nice). But that makes it hard to have plot_decision_region_slices be a wrapper for plot_decision_regions. Instead, I have plot_decision_region_slices fall back to using plot_decision_regions if there are only 2 training features.

Good point. I think what we could do is to add a parameter to the `plot_decision_plots function` to select 2 features from the X array. E.g., let's say

```python
plot_decision_regions(..., feature_index=None)
```
And if ` feature_index=None`, it would select the first to features by default, i.e.,`(0, 1)`, if the dataset is 2D, and `(0,)` if the dataset is 1D. If `X.shape[1] > 2`, it would use `(0, 1)` as well but add those filler values for the other features. This just has to be documented well so users don't get confused (I got a lot of emails where people were asking why the function doesn't work on their multidimensional datasets and thought there was a bug). Further, a user can then specify other slices than `(0, 1)`, e.g., `(0, 2`, ..., `(2, 4)`, ...  etc. 

Or in other words, I guess there's no good way around making the `plot_decision_region_slices` work without the modification above, right? What would work though is to let `the plot_decision_regions` function plot 1 custom slice (e.g., `(0, 1)` or `(0, )` by default) and have the `plot_decision_region_slices` as a wrapper function to plot a grid of slices.

What do you think?

* Would you recommend moving any other `utils` to the `basic` module? (ie. bold, italic, etc.). * Would it be alright if the display_page_number() method was kept static in the Head class? In some instances I don't add the page number to the actual header, but to another element (such as a MiniPage) first before adding it to the header/footer.

Hi @chrizrobert 

Thank you for your contribution. I'm really sorry for taking so long to respond, I've been a bit overwhelmed from many fronts.

I liked your use of `str.maketrans`. That's not something you see everyday.

Unfortunately there are three deadly reasons for why I can't accept this pull request:

- This library strives for supporting international keyboard layouts. `Shift+1` is not always `!`, so we can't have a hard-coded mapping.
- `str.upper` handles more cases than just ASCII letters; for example, `'á'.upper() == 'Á'`. It's not feasible to include all such cases.
- The library is on it's way to implementing this functionality on the OS-dependent backends. `Shift+1` already reports as `!` on Windows, for example.

In the end your pull request is solid, but you got unlucky and picked a problem that was already being solved, and is incredibly tricky to get right (internationalization).

I'm sorry for the delay and for closing this PR, but I would love to receive more contributions from you in the future.

Hi - 
While it's different from the previous version, I think that the current behavior is actually the correct behavior.

The reason is that `Interval` objects store everything as strings with the exception of start and stop coords. It's trivial for pybedtools to convert the float to string when creating an `Interval` object out of the tuple. But when accessing that data it would be a string. That is,
```python
# hypothetical: convert float to string upon Interval creation
x = Interval("chr1", 10, 100, 5.0)
x[4]
# returns string "5.0", not 5.0
```
That implicit conversion could be surprising for users. It's more explicit for the caller to do the string conversion. That is, change your example to this:

```python
intervalList = [('chrm_01', 10, 20, '0.711'),('chrm_01', 15, 30, '0.66'),('chrm_01', 40, 60, '0.011'),('chrm_01', 50, 80, '0.81221')]
pybedtools.BedTool(intervalList)
```

Technically, this is possible by including an InlineImage in the `footer_text` of a page template configuration. Unfortunately, inline images can currently not be specified in a `.rtt` file. You would have to create a template configuration by creating a Python source file and instantiating `TemplateConfiguration`. Since this also means you cannot use `rinoh` to render your document, this is not very convenient.

I'll look into adding support for specifying inline images in `.rtt` files. It could look something like this:

```
[page]
footer_text = IMAGE('images/footer.pdf', width=0.5in, baseline=20%)
              '\t'
              '{PAGE_NUMBER}'
              '\t'
              '{SECTION_NUMBER(2)}' (section number)
              '{nbsp}{nbsp}{nbsp}'
              '{SECTION_TITLE(2)}' (section title)
```

This would place the image at the left in the footer, the page number in the center and the section number and title at the right.

> Yes. The key thing here is what slide masters and slide layouts you have
> available to you at run time.
>
> When you add the slides having a different theme, their slide master and
> slide layouts are automatically added to the file too. You just don't see
> them unless you go looking for them in the Slide Master view. Once that's
> done, you get rid of the slides you added so you can start with an empty
> presentation. Deleting the slides does *not* remove the new masters and
> layouts. Once they're in they're there to stay unless you delete them
> manually. All this is done "by hand" using PowerPoint, just to get your
> template file configured correctly.
>
> There are other ways to do it--any method that gets the masters and
> layouts in there is fine--but this way is probably the quickest.
>
> What you're really looking for is how the starting presentation looks in
> Slide Master view. In regular Slide view mode, it should be empty when
> you're done. Slide Master view is also where you can figure out what the
> index of a particular master or layout is; just remember to start counting
> at 0 instead of 1.
>
> Note there is at least one other part involved, the theme{n}.xml files.
> Those specify the palette and fonts used by the master and layouts for that
> theme, and perhaps a few other details. Those get added automatically by
> this process. I just mention them here for anyone who might take a more
> direct approach :)

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division, print_function, unicode_literals

import requests
import justext


def main():
    response = requests.get(
        "http://www.efinancialcareers.de/jobs-Deutschland-Berlin-"
        "Spezialist-in_Banksteuerung_Meldewesen_MaRisk.id00903684"
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    page = response.content
    any_lang_stop_words = get_all_stop_words()

    paragraphs = justext.justext(page, any_lang_stop_words, 99, 100, 0.1, 0.32, 0.2, 200, True)
    for paragraph in paragraphs:
        if not paragraph.is_boilerplate:
            print(paragraph.text.encode("utf-8"))

    return 0


def get_all_stop_words():
    stop_words = set()
    for language in justext.get_stoplists():
        stop_words.update(justext.get_stoplist(language))

    return stop_words


if __name__ == "__main__":
    exit(main())
```

I'd like it if this can support the following as a testing tool (not for benchmarks):

Suppose, I have 4 filters (as `GET` params) in an API endpoint. I want all of them, and their combinations to be tested with their possible values and values which are not allowed.

So if I had filter params: 
    max_age (integers), name (regex, string), country (regex, string), employed (boolean, true, false).

I want a way to generate all or some combinations of these from a provided list (return value checks are not necessary). 

Maybe something like:

```
- test:
  - combinator: {name: 'filter', type:'form', params: {max_age: [10, 20, 30, 40, 50], name: ['Doe', 'Jane']}}
  - url: '/api/v1/person.json?$filter'

# `type` can be json/xml/form so it could be used to generate test data
# This would in turn create test values for `$filter` like:
# max_age=10&name=Doe
# max_age=20&name=Doe
# ... and so on ... 
```

This may not always be necessary, because the above example looks like something that a testing suite DB layer itself should handle. But there are other areas where this can be quite helpful (params that have less to do with the database and are some business logic things). Regarding the naming of the new hidden functions:  I think we should leave the trailing underscore in; if we don't, and NAIF publishes C wrappers (e.g. txtopn_c wrapper for txtopn_), then we have already used the name dafonw for the hidden function and can't re-use it without breaking anyone's existing code.

An alternative would be to use _hidden(...) or _f(...); the latter acknowleges that any _-trailing funtion originates from FORTRAN source.

@bauerj statistically significant? Yes. User experience significant? Eh, maybe? On my beefy Mac Pro these changes give me about 1000 extra txes/s in the log output and cut down my from-genesis-to-today estimate from around _11h 35m_ to _10h 11m_.

These changes are taking some of the lowest hanging fruit from some of the hardest hit sections if you look at a profiler. I'm exploring some changes that are either harder to implement or harder to test that would make a more obvious dent. In no particular order, those are:
* **Use unix domain socket instead of TCP loopback to a local full node's RPC port**
  * TCP is sloooow
  * This is actually an easy addition, but we're probably going to have to wait until Core 0.16 depending on the libevent project's roadmap. Tracked in bitcoin/bitcoin#9919
* **uvloop option for remote RPC**
  * Not sure how involved this will be.
  * My only concern is if it will be compatible out of the box with the custom executor we use in controller.
* **Explore using a `memoryview` for slicing intensive operations.**
  * The unfortunate reality with `memoryview` is that after slicing things up, you eventually need to retrieve those bytes for hashing and other operations. This makes it less useful to test the optimization on a statement by statement basis. You need to test it's effect from start to finish.
* **Explore using `bytesarray` or `deque` for append-intensive operations.**
  * The tx processor for segregated witness on litecoin and bitcoin would be a good candidate for experimentation

I wouldn't be surprised if @kyuupichan has already been exploring a couple of these.

I think we need two related mechanisms on the Sphinx side:
- The alias role/directive which in some form renders a declaration without redeclaring it. My current thought is that it should basically be like how forward declarations look (but some customization could be done via options to directives). The name of the entity would be a link to the actual declaration.
- The synopsis directive, which requires the ability to list all symbols in a given scope. Each declaration is the essentially rendered with the alias directive/role. This is a can of worms with respect to options as people might want arbitrary filters for which entities are listed, and how they are sorted.

I have an old prototype for both features somewhere that I will try to make work again.

That's basically the right solution, yes. I'm now detecting whether the inventory points to a file or dir. If it points to a dir, I don't use `dirname()`, as it strips off the last part of the path, which we don't want.

I was confusing myself with regards to the way your inventory is built up. I expected ansible-cmdb to look for `host_vars` and `group_vars` in `inventories/inventory_prod/clients1/host_vars`. As in, relative to the `hosts` file. But ansible-cmdb recursively scans for files when given a directory as the argument to `-i`. This is why it's finding your `hosts` file. Just as a precautionary remark: if you have `host_vars` or `group_vars` at `inventory-prod/client1/host_vars`, ansible-cmdb will *not* detect those.

I've pushed a fix to the master branch that now properly determines the path to `host_vars` and `group_vars`. I've also added some additional debugging information:

```

Thanks to @cyli, we tracked this down to a tqdm bug, where it's somehow misidentifying your operating system, and then fails to handle a return value that's None.

Could you run this snippet and see if it fails on your box?

```python
import tqdm, sys, time

progress = tqdm.tqdm(file=sys.stderr, unit="B", unit_scale=True, total=100)
with progress:
    for i in range(100):
        progress.update(i)
        time.sleep(0.01)
print "exiting"
```
If it's the bug we think it is, that will fail with something like:

```
% python /tmp/test-tqdm.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/test-tqdm.py", line 3, in <module>
    progress = tqdm.tqdm(file=sys.stderr, unit="B", unit_scale=True, total=100)
  File "/Users/warner/stuff/tahoe/magic-wormhole/ve/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tqdm/_tqdm.py", line 677, in __init__
    ncols = _environ_cols_wrapper()(file)
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable
Exception AttributeError: "'tqdm' object has no attribute 'disable'" in <object repr() failed> ignored
```

The tqdm object has a `__del__` which calls `self.close()`, which seems unwise, because the object may be halfway deleted by the time it tries to call itself, and `self.close()` checks for `self.disable`. So if an exception is raised during `__init__` (by that `_environ_cols_wrapper` being None, because it misrecognized your OS), when the object goes out of scope, `__del__` is called, then `close()`, then an exception happens.

And then our use of `@inlineCallbacks` makes the error get displayed weird.

The root problem is probably in tqdm._utils, in this code:

```python
from platform import system as _curos
CUR_OS = _curos()
IS_WIN = CUR_OS in ['Windows', 'cli']
IS_NIX = (not IS_WIN) and any(
    CUR_OS.startswith(i) for i in
    ['CYGWIN', 'MSYS', 'Linux', 'Darwin', 'SunOS', 'FreeBSD', 'NetBSD'])
```

and then this:

```python
def _environ_cols_wrapper():  # pragma: no cover
    """
    Return a function which gets width and height of console
    (linux,osx,windows,cygwin).
    """
    _environ_cols = None
    if IS_WIN:
        _environ_cols = _environ_cols_windows
        if _environ_cols is None:
            _environ_cols = _environ_cols_tput
    if IS_NIX:
        _environ_cols = _environ_cols_linux
    return _environ_cols
```

I *think* that on your machine, neither IS_WIN nor IS_NIX are True, so `_environ_cols_windows()` returns None, and `_tqdm.__init__` can't deal with that.

I've worked around this in the capture and data-export by replacing occurrences of is_processing_complete() with the following:

'''python
	def wait_for_processing_complete(self, timeout=5):
		"""Wraps self.is_processing_complete with more lenient waiting.

		There is some race condition in the Saleae software!

		Args:
			timeout: (int) seconds before timeout
		Raises:
			IOError: if timed out
		"""
		tic = time.time()
		time.sleep(0.2)
		done = False
		while not done:
			try:
				# Check for timeout in outer loop
				if time.time() - tic > timeout:
					self.capture_stop()
					raise IOError('Saleae timeout {} reached.'.format(timeout))

				while not self.is_processing_complete():
					# Check for timeout in inner loop
					if time.time() - tic > timeout:
						self.capture_stop()
						raise IOError('Saleae timeout = {}sec reached!'.format(timeout))
					time.sleep(0.2)
				done = True
			except self.CommandNAKedError:
				# This exception is raised if processing of capture is not complete on the
				# side of the analyzer. This is expected for the first few calls, so we
				# simply ignore it until is_processing_complete returns true
				pass
'''

And for file export, I use 'os' to poll for the output file existing and having the same size in two successive samples, using a similar timeout scheme.

> So you didn't realize it, but your file was always being written in binary mode.

Sorry, I should have been more explicit. When referring to binary vs text mode I was talking about the flag used to _open_ the file (eg, "w" vs "wb"). Simple test (results are on my last comment):

```python
#import unicodecsv as csv
import csv

f = open('myfile.csv', 'w')
#f = open('myfile.csv', 'wb')
ret = csv.writer(f).writerow(('a', 'b', '\u017d'))
f.close()
```

Py3 text-mode files have associated encoding `f.encoding == 'UTF-8'` (that's the default), while binary-mode files are just a stream of bytes. I reckon, that's why Py3 csv module does not have `encoding=` anymore. It is handled at file-level.

In the pep440 branch I'm aiming to make any combination of pre-, post- and dev releases possible, and to allow for automatic numbering if that makes sense, and to leave it up to the discretion of the programmer what kind of versioning scheme to use. 

I agree that the spirit of PEP440 is more restrictive (especially considering that setuptools/pip now throw warnings at you if you make use of normalization, which is specifically allowed in the PEP), but it's my opinion that the PEP should define the technical aspects such as parsing and ordering, and leave the semantics up to the programmer. 

So I'd like to choose for flexibility in the tooling, and to trust that a programmer won't release a complete rewrite in a post release, based on the "responsible adults" principle.

Thanks @amineparis ! Glad you like Django Extensions and that you find it such a great value.

Django Extensions was always meant for things that are not easily included into Django itself and as a testing ground for inclusion.

Although the later rarely happens I admit. If you take a look at two extensions that might be good candidates to include into Django itself like shell_plus and sqldiff, they both need a lot of work and refactoring before they could make it in. Other commands like runserver_plus are not likely for inclusion since they depend on third party packages.

Django Extensions does not adhere to the same rigor as Django although we still strive for quality and stability. This means we can include (or throw out) things sooner / more easily then Django can. It also means that Django does not have to suffer the burden of maintaining these things.

Lastly a lot of the code is meant for development only. The benefit of separate projects is that people can install Django Extensions in development and leave it out of production in most cases.

So I think there is a place for Django Extensions besides Django. 

That said we are always working with Django to make sure Django Extensions is compatible with the latest Django versions and to move extensions into Django itself or if Django maintainers see a need for it move contribs from Django to Django Extensions.

It's currently not possible.

If somebody could write a clean patch for this I'm happy to merge it.

Not only deleted: suppose you have a package, already installed, and from *now on* you decide to use a registry.xml in an upgradeStep for a specific feature.

If you have a browser view that asks for this new registry value and you didn't run your upgradeStep before rendering, you need the `try/except` as well or you get an error.

We're +1 for this approach from @hvelarde:

```python
enabled_portal_types = api.portal.get_registry_record(record, [])
```

This way, everyone who are still using `InvalidParameterError` will not break, but those who want to get rid of the `try/except` will benefit.

OK this seems to be one of the tricky things of S3.  When creating a bucket in a specific region, you'd better create your S3 resource with SAME region beforehand.  Otherwise, although the bucket creation would still succeed, some other operations (such as delete) will not be able to redirect to the correct region, when you are using a DNS-incompatible bucket name. The correct pattern looks like this.

    import boto3


    region = 'us-east-1'  # or 'us-west-2' or 'eu-west-1' or etc.
    s3 = boto3.resource('s3', region_name=region)
    bucket = s3.Bucket('testing.dotsinbucket')  # See the footnote below

    # See http://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/rande.html#s3_region
    if region == 'us-east-1':
        bucket.create()
    else:
        bucket.create(CreateBucketConfiguration={'LocationConstraint':region})

    # Another tip: You can enable log for debug
    boto3.set_stream_logger('botocore')

    bucket.delete()

    # By the way, you may not reuse a bucket name which was occupied in different
    # region recently. So you may need to adjust bucket names slightly in this test.

I'll just add that we understand the desire to have a more colorful or verbose output, but ...
When adding such features, one needs to consider the use-case outside of our own.
The main purpose of shiv is to be used as a part of other build tooling that is itself a part of the continuous integration pipeline. As such, it does not need to produce the verbose output. In fact, other tools reading its output prefer it that way. Also, as @sixninetynine points out, the overall *dependency footprint* of the whole build ecosystem needs to stay small.

On the other hand, the users who use shiv as just a command-line utility they directly interact with may want to have more output and colorize it perhaps. The usual way to resolve these two conflicting wishes is to use *extra* in the Python package specification. That way you can ask to install _shiv_ with an _extra_ to colorize it or make it verbose. For example `shiv[colorize]` or `shiv[verbose]`, but other users would not get this by default. The default would remain simple build pipeline tool with small number of dependencies.

The extras can be added using `extras_require` feature in the `setup()` call in _setup.py_. That sounds sufficiently easy change to make, but it isn't really. The code needs to be written in such a manner to add the new features if the extras are present, but to gracefully skip them if they are not.

`SELECT * FROM X` triggers Hive's fetch task optimization (`hive.fetch.task.conversion`). This means that the query is declared successful pretty much immediately and starts spitting out data (which is what a fetch task means). If you disable fetch task conversion or use something requiring a mapreduce job, then you'll see asynchronous progress.

`html_html` looks weird to me as a `type=` argument value.
Also, how are we going to document this? "html", "xml" are easy to understand for users. "html_html" is harder: _well, it's for HTML sure, but it only adds internal methods (that are not exposed so you don't need to care_.
As I see it, it'll be there "for those who know", who know that `self.root` gives access to the underlying lxml parsed doc, which is handy indeed ([and even used in scrapy linkextractors](https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/blob/master/scrapy/linkextractors/lxmlhtml.py#L49)). How about exposing `self.root` explicitly (with docs) under another name with "lxml" in it?

In the end, I would lean towards what @eliasdorneles said in https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/pull/41#issuecomment-228135980 , and would be interested in performance comparison, and if it's really close, make `lxml.html.HTMLParser` the default parser.

Another option is to add a `parser=` argument "for those who know what they're doing", so that we could even support [html5lib](http://lxml.de/html5parser.html) and [beautifulsoup](http://lxml.de/elementsoup.html) parsers for cases where lxml chokes.

not that I'm going to make this any less hazy but we are seeing the same error:

```AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'id'
  File "vip_filter/worker.py", line 93, in <module>
    worker.process_stream()
  File "pykafkaworker/worker.py", line 54, in process_stream
    self._report_offsets(message)
  File "pykafkaworker/worker.py", line 93, in _report_offsets
    self._consumer.commit_offsets()
  File "pykafka/balancedconsumer.py", line 720, in commit_offsets
    return self._consumer.commit_offsets()
  File "pykafka/simpleconsumer.py", line 410, in commit_offsets
    self._offset_manager.id)```

It turns out that kafka-python allows this by [promising to fetch the given offset next](https://github.com/mumrah/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/consumer/kafka.py#L206), not by actually committing the given offset to kafka. This is a problem because it creates a mismatch between the offset stored in kafka and the offset stored in the consumer; this is contrary to the design we've tried to maintain in pykafka so far.
Given that we'd like to commit offsets as soon as possible to kafka, we could try simply sending the user-supplied offsets to kafka during `reset_offsets` in an `OffsetRequest`. Unfortunately, the `OffsetRequest` only accepts timestamps, not actual offset values. When supplied with a timestamp it doesn't like, the request returns with an error code of `0` and an empty list for the offset. This seems to be a bug or at least a poor design choice in kafka.
It occurs to me that we could allow the user to supply either timestamps or actual offset values to `reset_offsets`. If the request succeeds and returns an offset (the dual error condition that we apparently have to check), we're all good. If it returns the empty offset list, we can simply set the consumer's internal offset to the supplied value and continue on. This will mirror the behavior of kafka-python and provide a bit more versatility.

@lboudard, when you're dealing with multi-lang components (like in streamparse), each executor (JVM thread) maps to a single Python process. Increasing the number of tasks per executor wouldn't buy you anything (and would likely make things slower), because Python can't actually process two tuples simultaneously.  If I ever get the time to finish pystorm/pystorm#24, we would have asynchronous components that would offer higher throughput in these scenarios.

@siddarth-jay if we were to magically combine channels, the best method to do that would be using the luminance channel available via `skimage.rgb2gray`. But this is not the only way to map RGB data to a grayscale image, so imposing it magically is a little bit dangerous. On Wed, 5 Aug 2015, Owen Synge wrote:
> Ok I try to be clearer:
> 
> Assuming you have a freshly wiped test cluster, with authorisation and repositories setup, but have previously setup a ceph.conf file, why do you
> once again have to run these commands in the correct order:
> 
> ceph-deploy install ceph-node2 ceph-node3 ceph-node4
> ceph-deploy mon create-initial
> ceph-deploy --overwrite-conf rgw create ceph-node3:onr
> 
> When all the data needed to define the commands is already available in ceph.conf.

Because ceph.conf doesn't describe a cluster.  It should be a minimal set 
of configs to bootstrap things (like, which mon addrs to try to get the 
*actual* state of the cluster, as maintained by the mons.)  It certainly 
wouldn't know anything about your rgw or mds or other instances.  We used 
to do that (defined all daemon in ceph.conf) but it's ungainly and 
difficult to maintain.

> If you had a model, that gathered the desired state, a way to establish the current state, a way to compare states, and a way to apply states,
> this could just be:
> 
> ceph-deploy apply-cfg
> 
> Essentially this is the strength of MVC. Also if you ran a redundant reapply ceph-deploy could simply gather the state and be able to tell you no
> operations are needed.
> 
> This is not hard to do, with the MVC design pattern.

...and if you want to take a cluster description and have a single button 
that makes it so, use chef or puppet -- that's what those tools are 
designed to do.

ceph-deploy is designed to do what you ask it to, and nothing more.  I am 
more likely to run a subset of the above commands than all of them in 
sequence.  e..g, I will upgrade with ceph-deploy install.  I will add a 
single osd with ceph-deploy osd create.  I will add a single rgw with 
ceph-deploy rgw create.  If you want to repeat the same sequence over 
again after wiping the cluster repeatedly (is this a test environment?)
then you can put it in a script to save typing...
this looks good to me
@trhoden  rebased to master to make this clearer for https://github.com/ceph/ceph-deploy/pull/355

```Python
try:

                                 len(value.shape),len(self.shape)))
    valid = all([a==b for a,b in zip(self.shape,value.shape)] if b is not None)
except AttributeError:
    raise mm.ValidationError("{} is not a valid array, does not have "
                                "attribute `shape`.".format(value))
if not valid:
    raise mm.ValidationError("Array shape {} does not match required "
                            "shape {}.".format(value.shape,

> 1. How many of these @property-s are copy-pasta?

Basically all of them. I made a slight change to `view_use_legacy_sql` to use the `type` property to check if a table is a view. This is because the `query` property of the view is not included in the table list response, so the type property is the only way to check if the table is a view when `useLegacySql` is left at the default value.

> 2. I don't think the class name really conveys what it is (a read-only version of Table).

It being read-only is more of a side-effect than the primary purpose of the class.

> 3. Is there a reason you might be opposed to just subclassing Table or just an arbitrary decision to make a new class?

The primary purpose of `TableListItem` is to indicate that there is a different set of properties available on the list response than a full `Table` object. Notable properties that aren't there include schema and total rows (#4373).

A sub-class would allow this object to be used in any place a table is allowed, such as update or create requests, which is not something I want people to be doing (because not all properties are present).

> 4. Are you opposed to adding a read_only attribute / member to Table?

I hadn't really considered it, because it wouldn't solve the problem of `schema` or `num_rows` not being populated from a list operation.

I think the issue may be a thread leak. When it occurs for me, the CPU is spending all its time in `treading.wait`. I noticed that the thread count gets really high too. I believe the problem is here: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/_consumer.py#L231 in `_blocking_consume`. 

According to [this comment](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/subscriber/_consumer.py#L56), gRCP creates a thread to run the request generator. When there's a `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED` exception and the policy ignores it and the `while True` loop starts over. It creates a new request generator which (somewhere in gRPC, I assume) creates a new thread. The number of threads waiting in `threading.wait` builds up until it starts eating all the CPU.

I tried moving lines 231 and 232 (creating the request and response generators) to just before the `while True` loop and let it run all night. It's been fine. Thread count and CPU are stable. And errors are still occurring and getting ignored. 

Perhaps worth mentioning: I use a custom policy which inherits the default but also ignores status `UNAVAILABLE`. Handled just like `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED` (ignored) but `UNAVAILABLE` occurs about every 2 minutes for me.

Alright, I figured out what's going on here.

The `build-support/pants_venv` helper script passes the `--quiet` flag to `pip`.  This sets `pip`'s log handler to filter all messages below log level `CRITICAL`.  However, the useful information that would have helped you debug this was being emitted at log level `INFO`, so it was swallowed by the `--quiet` flag.  If you remove the flag like this:

```diff
diff --git a/build-support/pants_venv b/build-support/pants_venv
index 70ea909..8594030 100755
--- a/build-support/pants_venv
+++ b/build-support/pants_venv
@@ -46,7 +46,7 @@ function activate_pants_venv() {
     create_venv || die "Failed to create venv."
     activate_venv || die "Failed to activate venv."
     for req in ${REQUIREMENTS[@]}; do
-      pip install --quiet ${pip_extra} -r "${req}" || \
+      pip install ${pip_extra} -r "${req}" || \
         die "Failed to install requirements from ${req}."
     done
     touch "${BOOTSTRAPPED_FILE}"
```

I'm guessing that the noisy output at the `INFO` log level was the reason that `pants` originally supplied the `--quiet` flag in the first place.

Near the bottom of that you'll see that it has a slightly more helpful error message (that the `Python.h` include is missing) that was being logged at `INFO` level.  It's not perfect, but a somewhat experienced developer could then figure out that they need `python-dev`.

So the fix I'm proposing is two-fold:

* I'm going to open a pull request against `pip` to raise the log level for that particular error message
* I'm going to also open a review board against `pants` to guard the `pip` command with a more helpful error message so that it suggests possibly installing Python development headers to resolve the issue

After a lot of poking, I finally managed to get a stacktrace out - https://travis-ci.org/pantsbuild/pants/builds/379165187

It turns out Gitignore keeps a thread-local cache, as an optimisation, which can't be disabled. It looks like the way thread_local stores thread IDs isn't fork-safe. I don't know why this isn't surfacing on more modern versions of OSX, or indeed on older rusts.

I modified Gitignore to bypass the thread_local cache, and it turns out the standard regex crate, which it uses, also uses a thread_local cache, so also triggers the same issue: https://travis-ci.org/pantsbuild/pants/builds/379216270

This was uncomfortably difficult to diagnose. I will put together some changes to make it easier in the future. I don't know what to do about fixing it, though.

It's not about the codeUri attribute for a lambda function (because as you stated, it's an AWS limitation, the bucket has to be in the same region as the function) but the DefinitionUri which aim to a S3 location containing the Swagger document for an API.
I've fixed this by using the solution provided by dinvlad here: https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/8#issuecomment-290212883
It fixed my bucket location problem and allowed me to use the Fn::Sub in my Swagger file for the function arn.

However, I think it's still an issue not be able to have the Swagger file in a different region than the one in which we deploy the stack?
Yes this is a known limitation. But why do you care though? !Ref and !Sub give the same result.

> What is there to change?

To make PlatformCollector default to not registering itself with the default registry, except for the default instance. So:

```
    def __init__(self, registry=None, platform=None):
...
PLATFORM_COLLECTOR = PlatformCollector(core.REGISTRY)
```

This is more explicit and means someone creating their own PlatformCollector doesn't end up with one already registered by accident.

I closed immediately after I realized that is not a issue about the library and especially about that method.

When you open a device, the hidapi library detaches it from the OS kernel in order to access and read it.

As far as I understand there's no way to monitor a device (let say mouse)  while using it for regular use (doing what you do normally with a mouse or a keyboard)

To regain the ability to use the device is to reattach it to the kernel, indeed closing it and so loosing the ability to monitor it.

> Hmm, the port is hard-coded in the docker file. Is there a better way to do this if the user wants to use a different port without needing to change the Dockerfile?

This is perfectly fine to hard-code this port to the default value. Because, when services run inside a container, they don't conflict with the ports of host or other containers. If the service is to be exposed on a different port, user can simply map the internal container port to any host port at run time. However, if this is something we must need to provide a way to allow overwriting, environment variable would be one way to go. Other than that, without any change it is still possible to run the service on a different port and map it, but in that case this port will also be exposed from the container, though it wont have anything to serve on it.

> I think this change should have gone in a branch other than master, @ibnesayeed , until we figured it out.

It is a non-conflicting unobtrusive addition to the repo, hence a branch would have been unnecessary. With this being pushed to the master branch, it would be easier to fix those issues in the repo that are preventing it to be run in an isolated environment. We can easily build an image, test it, and fix things as necessary.

so I spent some time looking at this today, a few pieces of feedback / for discussion

(1) this feels to me like something that should be in `scripts/` rather than a command.
(2) this feels very complicated to me. like, "I read the code multiple times and I'm still not 100% sure what it's doing or how to use it" complicated. as this PR stands, I don't think anyone would actually be able to use this functionality unless you showed them how. it's possible that all it needs is a tutorial to go with it, but it's also possible that it's more complicated than it needs to be.

@nkeim, I think your description is entirely correct (although I have not checked the Blair-Dufresne code in depth)

The most logical choice for the length scale in `grey_dilation` would be `separation`. I cannot come up with any practical use to do it differently. The only thing is that it may lead to issues when combined with `maxsize` filtering (see the failed test `test_minmass_maxsize`, https://travis-ci.org/soft-matter/trackpy/jobs/128360102).

Basically, small features next to large features with size > maxsize will not be detected in the new algorithm, while they would be deteced in the old algorithm. The issue here is a wrong choice of `separation`: when it is much larger than feature `diameter` (as in the test), this leads to the issue.

So I would still go for `separation` and document the slight API change.

These are the answers I can provide:

> determine if the user has an ability, period, to authenticate to use the endpoint

This cannot be known until the user attempts activation.
Consider an endpoint with the simplest myproxy server setup to use local accounts with password auth.

I may be `sirosen@globus.org` as far as Globus and your application are concerned, but my account on the endpoint may be `stephen` with password `abc123!`.
Until I attempt myproxy activation and provide my local username and password, the Globus Transfer API cannot know whether or not I, the human user, have credentials to use that endpoint.

To varying degrees, that same model applies to the other activation types.

> determine if the user can (without extra work) do this. Right now I think the field "activated" as False would mean both "I can't connect to it without further authentication" but also "I can't connect period because I'm not a user of this resource." We would want to untangle those two things.

The `activated: False` field just tells you whether or not the endpoint is currently activated. It contains and implies no further information.

It is documented, along with the other endpoint document fields, here:
https://docs.globus.org/api/transfer/endpoint/

The Transfer API does not have any notion of who is allowed to use different endpoints beyond the various current activations which may be in effect.
If we want to go a layer deeper, it doesn't even know if the credentials from an endpoint are valid until it attempts to do a filesystem operation -- a misconfigured endpoint could be activated, but totally broken.

"I can't connect to it without further authentication" therefore describes *all endpoints at all times*, and we can't, as users of the API, know anything more than that.

> If the user can (one of the booleans above) provide a link that can take them through two factor (or other custom auth) under the same page control (so not need to open globus.org unless it's a nice redirect).

This is going to depend a lot on how much work you want to do to support different activation flows within your application.

You can build out code to do all of the different types of endpoint activation, but there's an awful lot of it.
You would need to decide which of the [available activation types](https://docs.globus.org/api/transfer/endpoint_activation/#activation_types) you want to support.

The easiest way is to send the user to
```
"https://www.globus.org/app/endpoints/{}/activate".format(endpoint_id)
```
in a new tab and let them activate there.

The Globus WebApp handles various activation cases including the [OAuth MyProxy](https://docs.globus.org/api/transfer/endpoint_activation/#oauth_myproxy) case, which is one of the more secure options.

It should be technically possible for you to implement OAuth MyProxy activation yourself, but
- That method isn't publicly documented. You would do best to submit a question at support@globus.org if you wanted to find out how.
  Adding documentation for it wouldn't be a bad call, but I don't know how easy it would be to completely and accurately document, especially since it may depend on the versions and behaviors of the OAuth server in use on the endpoint.
- Gut call: it's probably a lot of work. Redirecting to `www.globus.org/app/endpoints/<id>/activate` is easy and basically foolproof.

---

Final thought: autoactivation.

As a general practice, most applications attempt autoactivation before even moving on to other activation types.
Autoactivation covers the endpoint configurations in which the endpoint trusts the Globus Transfer service and has some mapping from Globus accounts to local user accounts.

To steal a snippet from the Globus CLI,
```
# client is a globus_sdk.TransferClient
res = client.endpoint_autoactivate(endpoint_id, if_expires_in=60)

if "AlreadyActivated" == res["code"]:
    formatted_print(res, simple_text=(
        "Endpoint is already activated. Activation "
        "expires at {}".format(res["expire_time"])))
    return
```

The Globus Tutorial endpoints all respond to autoactivation, if you want a test case.

A good starting point (and arguably a good stopping point) for an application is to attempt autoactivation and then, if that fails, redirect users to the Globus Webapp.
It may require an extra authentication step for users to login there, but the end result will likely be more reliable than any alternative.

If you have further questions that aren't tied to specific pieces of SDK functionality, I would recommend asking them at support@globus.org, as activation is a fairly expansive topic. Questions or issues with the activation, autoactivation, and get_activation_requirements functionality provided by the SDK's TransferClient, of course, belong here.

Again, the motivator here is for us to more seamlessly be able to add additional architectures.  I've been approached for genuine interest in solaris and AIX.  If there are small things we can do to reduce barriers for new platforms and expand the community, I think we should.

I've encountered this myself when switching platforms between osx and linux on a channel somewhere.

Also, anaconda.org has become a de facto standard in some ways.  It works as I've proposed no matter what.

    https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz/label/main/ANY/repodata.json

where `ANY` is *literally* anything in that path element.  It gives a 200 response with valid json.  My proposal here is much more loose.  But it enables expansion of conda while still balancing code implementation concerns.

Before merging this I also want to solicit input on the conda mailing list.

Well, a clunky work around that I found at https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/3a2erd/exception_catch_not_catching_everything/ is as follows:

```
import contextlib
import sys
import os

@contextlib.contextmanager
def nostderr():
    savestderr = sys.stderr
    sys.stderr = os.devnull
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        sys.stderr = savestderr

try:
    with nostderr():
        es.ping()
except (AttributeError, ConnectionRefusedError):
    print('Connection could not be established.')
    # react accordingly, e.g. with retrying or switching to a different host...
```

This suppresses the exceptions that are flying in the background when the connection could not be established so that you can concentrate on how you actually want to handle that case.

The issue is that `sys.stdout.writable()` return `False`. The issue does not occur in IPython as such.

Due to the separate channels, it is not possible to enforce that shell messages arrive after iopub messages. If kernels comply with the spec, `status: idle` message will be sent at the end of handling every request. This should be used to indicate that output is complete for a given parent. We can leave this open as a docs issue until we add that note to the spec.

> Does it work with lists of string functions? Lists of user functions? Mixed
lists?

Yes, this affects each function separately and should not change the behavior of inputting lists.

> does conflict with me specifying my own globals?

Only in the specific case of globals defined using the same names as functions that are being inputted, for instance:

    def foo(x, a, b):
        return a * x + b

    def bar(x, a, b):
        return 7

    fitter = sm.data.fitter(f=[foo, 'foo(x, a, b)'], p='a,b', g={'foo': bar})

In general, doing something like that would be a Very Weird Decision; however, it's not impossible that it's been done, so in its current state this would be a breaking change. The cleanest way to deal with this would be to issue a deprecation warning when a function is given with a name that's already in `self._globals` and either ignore the input or prompt the user for a different name. During the next major release, the deprecation warning would be removed and the warning would turn into an error. I'd be happy to implement either, but will leave the decision up to you.

Hi @prasad7nara, I'm happy to help. OpenSSL errors are always tricky to debug. I'm in the process of collecting common SSL errors that users encounter for an upcoming FAQ update, along with solutions to resolving those errors. I haven't seen a `[SSL] PEM lib` error before, so this will be a nice addition.

I would start by verifying that all of your key and certificate files are actually in PEM format. Open each file and verify that it has readable text headers and footers, something like these:

```
$ cat clientkey
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
...
-----END PRIVATE KEY-----
```

```
$ cat signed.crt
Certificate:
...
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
...
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
```

The certificates may or may not have the parsed `Certificate:` section before the actual certificate bytes. 

Verify that OpenSSL can actually load and display the contents of the key and certificate files. For the key file use:

```
$ openssl rsa -noout -text -in clientkey
```

For the certificate files use:

```
$ openssl x509 -noout -text -in signed.crt
$ openssl x509 -noout -text -in MyCA.crt
```

Using the output for the two `openssl x509` commands above, double check that the validity dates are valid for the current time. The current date and time should be between the `Not Before` and `Not After` dates.

Using the output from the `openssl rsa` command the `openssl x509` command for `signed.crt`, triple check that the `modulus` and `publicExponent` fields of the key match the `Modulus` and `Exponent` fields of the certificate. If these do not match, the key is not valid for the certificate.

Finally, from your config log it looks like you are running the PyKMIP client on Windows. I have never tested PyKMIP on Windows, so it is quite possible that there are PyKMIP bugs for that platform I haven't discovered yet. If you continue to have problems on Windows, I would recommend trying to use the PyKMIP client with your key and certificate files on a Linux machine, just to determine if the error you see still occurs.

Let me know if any of these debugging steps highlights a problem or resolves your issue. If none of these help, we'll have to dig a little deeper.

@Ichimonji10 proposed option is better, of course, but the issue is different:
because of [this](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/pull/260/files#diff-3822710a997c3f2f7c3e93e884af69caR2036) line we'll always have `self._owner_type == None`, so
```python
    if '_owner_type' in attrs:
        attrs['owner_type'] = attrs.pop('_owner_type')
```
will always create attribute `owner_type` (at least equal to `None`), even if it wasn't defined in initialized entity instance.
As a result, default `Host().update_payload()` will look like this `{'host': {'owner_type': None}}` instead of `{'host': {}}`. Of course, i can fix unit tests and pretend it's supposed to work like that, but that's just wrong way of solving the issue :smile: 
So imo something like this should be applied:
```python
    def get_values(self):
        """Correctly set the ``owner_type`` attribute."""
        attrs = super(Host, self).get_values()
        if '_owner_type' in attrs and attrs['_owner_type'] is not None:
            attrs['owner_type'] = attrs.pop('_owner_type')
        else:
            attrs.pop('_owner_type')
        return attrs
```

> Ah, it wasn't clear ay first glance that SFrame is part of Turi Create. With such a tight relationship between SFrame and Turi Create, will there also be pandas support?

As I mentioned, SFrame and Pandas data frames are already deeply compatible. The APIs working with SFrames allows us to push a lot of optimizations into the model creation process that would otherwise not be possible by using Pandas.

> At the risk of drifting off topic, what is the overlap between SFrame and pandas, and what are significant differences?

I tried outlining the key differences above. The overlap in terms of functionality is pretty high. The SFrame is pretty full featured and we plan to keep building on it and adding more things as we find gaps. The APIs are pretty similar so it should be quite natural to use them.

> Also, how does SFrame compare with the design goals and foundations of Dask? E.g.

Dask is another great project that we are aware of. It started around a year after the SFrame and at its core is a task scheduler that can help scale up Pandas using pure python primitives. It has some of the advantages of SFrame (parallelism, scale, interop with pandas etc.) but not all of them (can be extended to multiple languages, lazy evaluation, compression, interop with pandas etc.)

```python
grompp(
  f=MDP['sd.mdp', {
    'integrator': 'steep',
    'emtol': 10.0,
    'nsteps': 10000}],
  c='ions.gro',
  o='sd.tpr'
)
```

Or maybe yet without a template file:

```python
 MDP[{
  'integrator': 'steep',
  'emtol': 10.0,
  'nsteps': 10000
}]
```
Then a function `mdp()` becomes more elegant.

With these changes GromacsWrapper looks more like utilities than a complete library or framework, which is a gain in my opinion.

Work is underway on a library for your request.  It will be housed in the `fontv.libfv` module and will include a new `FontVersion` object for read/substring parsing/writes of font version strings.

I am supporting dev/rel substring testing on this end so that implementation of dev/rel (and associated git commit SHA1 strings if used in combination with the dev/rel indicators) checks on your end will be through `FontVersion` object boolean properties and as simple as:

```python
from fontv.libfv import FontVersion

fv = FontVersion("path/to/font")

if fv.contains_status:
    if fv.is_development:
        # handle development labeled font
    elif fv.is_release:
        # handle release labeled font
else:
    # raise warning about lack of DEV/REL status label in the font version string
```

There are currently optional parameters that support modification of the `DEV`, `RELEASE`, `[sha1]-dev`, `[sha1]-release` formatted string labels during instantiation of the `FontVersion` object if you intend to modify fonts via the library and want to use a different format, including order and substring combinations.  Each of the individual version substring data elements that exist at the time of the font read will be available in a combination of Python dictionaries, lists, and strings to combine, revise, and write out however you'd like. 

My plan is to support modification of the version string and font file writes for these updates through public methods on the `FontVersion` object.  This isn't implemented yet but will look something like this:

```python
fv.add_git_commit_sha1(status="development")
# fv.add_git_commit_sha1(status="release")  # alternate for release versions
fv.write_version_string()  # the font binary is now updated with the version string modification
```

You will also have access to the `fontTools.ttFont` object as a `FontVersion` object property to use for your own implementation of writes to the font files if that is a better approach for you.

Hopefully that sounds broad and flexible enough to address anything that you need to address in the version strings on your end.  I will push a pull request once I make a bit more progress this weekend so that you can see source details and weigh in with any recommendations from your perspective.

            """ to_categorical.
            Convert class vector (integers from 0 to nb_classes)

                y: `array`. Class vector to convert.

            y = np.asarray(y, dtype='int32')

            if len(y.shape) > 2:

Special casing is easy with a short wrapper function if that's desired, but it should not be up to pyfftw to make assumptions about what is meant by `writable =False`.

@jsha ok, so as you have probably seen, I added a license, we chose the same license as Certbots for convenience, I had to take a dependency out because it was incompatible. 

We wrote this to run in a web hosting environment, so it's made to be scalable, you can run multiple processes and it runs lengthy/blocking processes in threads. It's meant to run 24/7/365, so it's a daemon with quite a bit of error handling to prevent it from locking up. It's also very efficient in that it only refreshes *n* hours (default=2hrs) before the current staple expires.

Having said that, I think this doesn't quite match up with the "average certbot" user's requirements. So the way I see it is there are 3 ways to incorporate this in certbot.

1. We include it as a daemon as-is. We set some sane defaults for small scale use, 1 or 2 threads should work perfectly fine and the application is still very lean so no harm done.
2. We extract the main logic and make this part of the renew process, this seams to make sense because it's the least invasive way of including OCSP stapling for the "average certbot" user, though probably not the most common use case (I suspect it's Apache).
3. Not really incorporating but maybe you could simply reference to our project from yours for NGiNX and HAProxy users.

Also please note that we have no unittests because it's an application that just combines already existing components for the most part (this is not an excuse, I still do want to - and/or should have included some unittests). But for this kind of application functional tests seems to make more sense, but we didn't do this yet because it involves running an ocsp server etc.. By the way, if anyone wants to contribute to that, more than welcome! The reason for this explanation is that "incorporation as-is" seems to conflict with the testing coverage of your project.

So please let me know, what you think, and what you would like to do. If you would like to do any of the options outlined above, or you have a suggestion of your own, I would be more than happy to help out as much as I can.

I believe Certbot isn't the problem here. I suspect in the production environment (the one used without `--dry-run`) you had successfully issued a certificate for this name using the same Certbot account within the last 30 days. The authorization created from that issuance is reused and so no fresh `http-01` challenge is performed. Meanwhile, in the staging environment (the one used with `--dry-run`) I suspect you don't have a cached authorization and so a fresh `http-01` challenge is done and this underlying problem with your DNS becomes visible.

I suspect if you created a new Certbot account and tried to issue for this domain without `--dry-run` it would fail with the same error message as you see now.

If you share your domain name I can try and help you diagnose why some part of your DNS infrastructure for this domain is returning a SERVFAIL error.

@ryanlovett I think you misinterpret what `WebSocketHandler.open` is and when it is called. It is not called when a client opens a connection to a server (before GET Upgrade request is sent over this connection), it is called only after `WebSocketHandler` responds to Upgrade request!

This is done in `WebSocketProtocol13` class, which is instantiated by `WebSocketHandler`.
From `WebSocketHandler.get`:
```python
        self.ws_connection = self.get_websocket_protocol()
        if self.ws_connection:
            self.ws_connection.accept_connection()
```
and `get_websocket_protocol` is defined as:
```python
    def get_websocket_protocol(self):
        websocket_version = self.request.headers.get("Sec-WebSocket-Version")
        if websocket_version in ("7", "8", "13"):
            return WebSocketProtocol13(
                self, compression_options=self.get_compression_options())
#               ^^^^ passes self to WebSocketProtocol13, which is accepted as a `handler` in that class
```
`WebSocketProtocol13.accept_connection` invokes `WebSocketProtocol13._accept_connection`. Which answers to Upgrade request and calls the `open` callback:
```python
        self.handler.set_status(101)
        self.handler.set_header("Upgrade", "websocket")
        self.handler.set_header("Connection", "Upgrade")
        self.handler.set_header("Sec-WebSocket-Accept", self._challenge_response())
        self.handler.finish()

        self.handler._attach_stream()
        self.stream = self.handler.stream

        self.start_pinging()
        self._run_callback(self.handler.open, *self.handler.open_args,
                           **self.handler.open_kwargs)
```

I'd consider this a duplicate of #55 and perhaps we should shift discussion over there.  The question there is how to go forward with the public API to make it handle developers' use cases while being easy to understand.  My understanding is that `runScript(String)` was originally added to support running `jep someFile.py` from the command line against a script, just like `python someFile.py`.  So for the purposes of the public API, I'm not sure we should keep the name `runScript` for the method that you're trying to accomplish.  I also don't think we should accept an InputStream because we can't stream that to Python (though I haven't fully researched it) so Jep would just be reading the stream into a String anyway.

@CFAndy I'm glad the new version's working fine for you! Regarding the best placement for the "patching" code, I must say I don't know. I'm not a big fan of directly adding this to the `__init__.py` file. Instead, I would probably prefer creating a `notation_patch.py` file, put it under the `notation` package and import it in the `__init__.py` without forgetting to add the `BendPoint` class to the `__all__` variable. This solution works, but if the code you need to manually add to the metamodel becomes longer (for example, you need to implement some `create_...` method that are defined in the `notation` metamodel), I would probably prefer a little bit more complex solution but which would be more flexible.

I would use the `--user-module <path>` option from pyecoregen (you can look the description on the pyecoregen page). It should give something like this:
```bash
$ pyecoregen -e notation.ecore -o . --user-module "notation_behavior.mixins"
```
I would then create the `notation_behavior` package (name is not great), with a `mixins.py` module inside, and copy the code generated under `notation/notation_mixins.py.skel` inside `mixins.py`. This way, I could modify the mixin code and add all the behavior I need. In this module, I would also add the `BendPoint` class and the two `from_str(...), to_str(...)` function (but renamed).
Then, in this same package, I would create a `notation_patch.py` module, with this code

```python
import notation
from notation_behavior.mixins import from_str, to_str

notation.View.persistedChildren.name = 'children'
notation.View.children = notation.View.persistedChildren
notation.Diagram.persistedEdges.name = 'edges'
notation.Diagram.edges = notation.Diagram.persistedEdges

notation.RelativeBendpointList.from_string = from_str
notation.RelativeBendpointList.to_string = to_str
```

And finally, I would modify the `notation/__init__.py` by adding:

```python
from notation_behavior.mixins import BendPoint
import notation_behavior.notation_patch
__all__ = ['Edge', ..., 'BendPoint']
```
This solution is more complexe, but it gives you the ability to delete the generated code if you need to (for some reasons), without impacting too much your existing code. I didn't test it, so perhaps it requires some adjustments.

@zhogov: Unfortunately, we have no intention to support Python 2.6 generally with this module. (Though we could probably stand to make that more explicit if its not already explicitly stated.) So I don't expect we would modify the offending line unless there was some other compelling reason to do so.

For what its worth, it looks like we could do something like this to achieve the same sort of dictionary comprehension with Python 2.6 compatibility:

```
# Python 2.7+:
In [15]: {k: [request.headers[k]] for k in request.headers}
Out[15]:
{'Content-Type': ['application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=utf-8'],
 'Host': ['sts.amazonaws.com']}

# Python 2.6:
In [16]: dict((k, [v]) for k, v in request.headers.iteritems())
Out[16]:
{'Content-Type': ['application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=utf-8'],
 'Host': ['sts.amazonaws.com']}

```

So in https://github.com/hvac/hvac/pull/242/ I'm hoping to establish a different pattern for how auth (and ultimately secret methods) are organized. Given that, and assuming the new pattern being proposed doesn't raise any general concerns on your part, I'd love to see these GCP method additions follow that same sort of pattern. I.e., instead of adding new methods to the quite large `Client` class, we could add a `hvac.api.auth.gcp.Gcp` class to hold these methods. Since this is a new development without corresponding updates to our [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/hvac/hvac/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md), I figure we could either:

* Evaluate this PR's changes as-is once test coverage has been included. Assuming everything checks out, we could merge the changes as they stand and I would then be happy to refactor things before the next release. Since I'm hoping to reduce the amount of code in the `Client` class, I wouldn't want to cut a release including these methods in that class. (Once folks start calling the methods in one location, its tricky to migrate them to a different source file without risking breaking external usage relying on that status quo.)
* If you're down to follow the same sort of pattern being included with https://github.com/hvac/hvac/pull/242/ and update these changes to match, I'd be happy to help provide guidance or otherwise provide any clarification should anything be unclear.
